code: @Manual{,   title = {sumvar: Summarise Continuous and Categorical Variables, Check for Duplicates and Missing Data},   author = {Alexander Stockdale},   year = {2025},   note = {R package version 0.1},   url = {https://github.com/alstockdale/sumvar}, }

what: Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
title: Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
text: Summarises median, interquartile range, mean, standard deviation, confidence intervals mean plots density histogram. Default option density plot histogram can overriden options "bee" "box". function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble.

title: Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
text: data data frame tibble ... variable like summarise Default, additional arguments: density plot histogram Add option "box" show boxplot violin plot eg. data %>% dist_one(var, box) Add option "bee" show beeswarm plot eg. data %>% dist_one(var, bee)

title: Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
text: tibble summary variable median, interquartile range, mean, SD, confidence intervals mean (using Z distribution), plots.

title: Examine the distribution of a single continous variable — dist_one
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, age = rnorm(100, mean = 30, sd = 10)) dist_one(example_data, age) # Default, with histogram and density plot #> `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value with `binwidth`.  #> # A tibble: 1 × 10 #>       n median   p25   p75  mean    sd ci_lower ci_upper   min   max #>   <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1   100   30.9  26.4  36.2  30.7  10.5     28.6     32.8  3.88  57.6 dist_one(example_data, age, box) # Option to show a boxplot and violin plot  #> # A tibble: 1 × 10 #>       n median   p25   p75  mean    sd ci_lower ci_upper   min   max #>   <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1   100   30.9  26.4  36.2  30.7  10.5     28.6     32.8  3.88  57.6 dist_one(example_data, age, bee) # Option to show a beeswarm plot (using ggbeeswarm)  #> # A tibble: 1 × 10 #>       n median   p25   p75  mean    sd ci_lower ci_upper   min   max #>   <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1   100   30.9  26.4  36.2  30.7  10.5     28.6     32.8  3.88  57.6 summary <- dist_one(example_data, age) # Save summary statistics as a tibble. #> `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value with `binwidth`.  # Note that this function accepts a pipe input # eg. example_data %>% dist_one(age)

what: Explore a continuous variable, stratified by a grouping variable — dist_sum
title: Explore a continuous variable, stratified by a grouping variable — dist_sum
text: Summarises median, interquartile range, mean, standard deviation, confidence intervals mean produces density plot, stratified second grouping variable. Provides frequentist hypothesis tests comparison groups: T test Wilcoxon rank sum 2 groups, Anova Kruskall wallis test 3 groups. function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble.

title: Explore a continuous variable, stratified by a grouping variable — dist_sum
Explore a continuous variable, stratified by a grouping variable — dist_sum
text: data data frame tibble var variable like summarise grouping variable

title: Explore a continuous variable, stratified by a grouping variable — dist_sum
text: tibble summary variable frequency (n), number missing observations (n_miss), median, interquartile range, mean, SD, 95% confidence intervals mean (using Z distribution), density plots. Shows T test (p_ttest) Wilcoxon rank sum (p_wilcox) hypothesis tests two groups Anova test (p_anova) Kruskal-Wallis test (p_kruskal) three groups.

title: Explore a continuous variable, stratified by a grouping variable — dist_sum
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, age = rnorm(100, mean = 30, sd = 10),                               group = sample(c("a", "b", "c", "d"),                               size = 100, replace = TRUE)) dist_sum(example_data, age, group)  #> # A tibble: 4 × 14 #>   group     n n_miss median   p25   p75  mean    sd   min   max ci_lower #>   <chr> <int>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 a        27      0   31.2  23.7  37.3  31.1 10.3   12.4  55.6     27.3 #> 2 b        30      0   30.6  21.5  37.6  30.1 11.1   11.0  49.9     26.2 #> 3 c        18      0   32.2  27.2  39.8  32.7  8.77  14.9  50.4     28.7 #> 4 d        25      0   34.2  23.1  37.7  31.3 10.1   13.2  51.3     27.3 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: ci_upper <dbl>, p_anova <dbl>, p_kruskal <dbl> example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, age = rnorm(100, mean = 30, sd = 10),                              sex = sample(c("male", "female"),                              size = 100, replace = TRUE)) dist_sum(example_data, age, sex)  #> # A tibble: 2 × 14 #>   sex        n n_miss median   p25   p75  mean    sd   min   max ci_lower #>   <chr>  <int>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 female    43      0   29.9  19.7  35.2  29.7 11.5  10.2   51.6     26.2 #> 2 male      57      0   32.0  26.0  40.5  32.1  9.91  8.22  49.7     29.5 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: ci_upper <dbl>, p_ttest <dbl>, p_wilcox <dbl> summary <- dist_sum(example_data, age, sex) # Save summary statistics as a tibble.  # Note that this function accepts a pipe input # eg. example_data %>% dist_sum(age, group)

what: Count the number of duplicates in a variable — dup
title: Count the number of duplicates in a variable — dup
text: Provides integer value number duplicates found wihtin variable function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble. eg. example_data %>% dup(variable)

title: Count the number of duplicates in a variable — dup
Count the number of duplicates in a variable — dup
text: data data frame tibble var variable assess

title: Count the number of duplicates in a variable — dup
text: tibble number duplicates found, number missing values (NA), together percentages. Missing values considered duplicates, excluded calculation percentage duplicate values.

title: Count the number of duplicates in a variable — dup
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:200, age = round(rnorm(200, mean = 30, sd = 5), digits=0)) example_data$age[sample(1:200, size = 15)] <- NA  # Replace 15 values with missing. dup(example_data, age) #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   n_duplicate percent_duplicate n_missing percent_missing #>         <int>             <dbl>     <int>           <dbl> #> 1         159              85.9        15             7.5 # Note that this function accepts a pipe input # eg. example_data %>% dup(group)

title: Count missing values — miss
text: Provides frequency percentage missing values. Counts NA, "" character variables (empty character variables) missing. function accepts input dplyr pipe %>% returns results tibble. variables specified, returns list missing values entire data frame/ tibble. eg. data %>% miss(variable1, variable2)

title: Count missing values — miss
Count missing values — miss
text: data data frame tibble ... variable(s) assess missing data dp number decimal places percentages

title: Count missing values — miss
text: tibble number valid values (n_valid), missing values (n_missing), together percentages.

title: Count missing values — miss
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:200, age = round(rnorm(200, mean = 30, sd = 5), digits=0),                                           sex = sample(c("male", "female"),                                           size = 200, replace = TRUE),) example_data$age[sample(1:200, size = 15)] <- NA  # Replace 15 values with missing. example_data$sex[sample(1:200, size = 30)] <- ""  # Replace 30 values with empty character vectors. miss(example_data, id, age, sex, dp=1) #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   variable total n_missing pc_missing #>   <chr>    <int>     <int> <chr>      #> 1 id         200         0 0.0        #> 2 age        200        15 7.5        #> 3 sex        200        30 15.0       # Note that this function accepts a pipe input # eg. example_data %>% miss()

title: Pipe operator — %>%
text: See magrittr::%>% details.

title: Pipe operator — %>%
Pipe operator — %>%
text: lhs value magrittr placeholder. rhs function call using magrittr semantics.

title: Pipe operator — %>%
text: result calling rhs(lhs).

what: sumvar: Summarise Continuous and Categorical Variables, Check for Duplicates and Missing Data — sumvar-package
title: sumvar: Summarise Continuous and Categorical Variables, Check for Duplicates and Missing Data — sumvar-package
text: sumvar package speeds data analysis tasks, reviewing continuous categorical variables. sumvar aims bring ease simplicity "sum" "tab" functions stata, R. explore distribution single continuous variable use dist_one(). explore continuous variable stratified grouping variable use dist_sum(). summarise single categorical variable use tab1(), twoway table two categorical variables- use tab(). dist_sum() tab() include options performning frequestist hypothesis tests. Explore duplicates dup() missing values miss(). functions tidyverse/dplyr-friendly accept

title: sumvar: Summarise Continuous and Categorical Variables, Check for Duplicates and Missing Data — sumvar-package
text: Maintainer: Alexander Stockdale .stockdale@liverpool.ac.uk

what: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
text: Creates "n x n" cross-tabulation two categorical variables, row percentages. Includes options adding frequentist hypothesis testing. function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble. eg. example_data %>% tab(variable1, variable2)

title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
text: data data frame tibble variable1 first categorical variable variable2 second categorical variable test Optional frequentist hypothesis test, use test=exact Fisher's exact test=chi Chi squared

title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
text: tibble cross-tabulation frequencies row percentages

title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, group1 = sample(c("a", "b", "c", "d"),                                                   size = 100, replace = TRUE),                                                   group2= sample(c("male", "female"),                                                   size = 100, replace = TRUE)) example_data$group1[sample(1:100, size = 10)] <- NA  # Replace 10 with missing tab(example_data, group1, group2) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   group1 Frequency_female Frequency_male Percent_female Percent_male #>   <chr>             <int>          <int>          <dbl>        <dbl> #> 1 a                     9             10           47.4         52.6 #> 2 b                    13             15           46.4         53.6 #> 3 c                    13             13           50           50   #> 4 d                     9              8           52.9         47.1 #> 5 NA                    6              4           60           40   summary <- tab(example_data, group1, group2) # Save summary statistics as a tibble. # Note that this function accepts a pipe input # eg. example_data %>% tab(group)

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
text: Summarises frequencies percentages categorical variable. function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble. eg. example_data %>% tab1(variable)

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
text: data data frame tibble variable categorical variable like summarise dp number decimal places percentages (default=2)

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
text: tibble frequencies percentages

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, group = sample(c("a", "b", "c", "d"),                                                   size = 100, replace = TRUE)) example_data$group[sample(1:100, size = 10)] <- NA  # Replace 10 with missing tab1(example_data, group)  #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   Category Frequency Percent #>   <chr>        <int> <chr>   #> 1 a               30 30.0    #> 2 c               23 23.0    #> 3 d               19 19.0    #> 4 b               18 18.0    #> 5 NA              10 10.0    #> 6 Total          100 100.0   summary <- tab1(example_data, group) # Save summary statistics as a tibble.  #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   Category Frequency Percent #>   <chr>        <int> <chr>   #> 1 a               30 30.0    #> 2 c               23 23.0    #> 3 d               19 19.0    #> 4 b               18 18.0    #> 5 NA              10 10.0    #> 6 Total          100 100.0   # Note that this function accepts a pipe input # eg. example_data %>% tab1(group)
